explanation: SET NOCOUNT is explicitly OFF, causing row-count messages to be sent to the client for every statement and increasing network overhead.
SET XACT_ABORT is OFF, allowing certain runtime errors to leave an open transaction partially applied and potentially causing data inconsistency.
SELECT * on Sales.Orders couples the caller to every current and future column, risking schema-change failures and unnecessary I/O.
SELECT TOP (10) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary, non-deterministic set of rows that can change between executions.
DISTINCT is applied to OrderID, which is the primary key and already guaranteed unique, adding a wasteful sort/hash operation.
WITH (NOLOCK) permits reading uncommitted, torn, or duplicated rows and bypasses all locking mechanisms.
YEAR(o.OrderDate) wraps the indexed date column in a function, preventing the optimizer from using an index seek on OrderDate.
Comparing int CustomerID to nvarchar @CustomerIdText forces implicit conversion of the column side, breaking index usage and causing type-conflict errors.
@CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID prevents parameter sniffing and forces a scan because the optimizer cannot estimate cardinality reliably.
LIKE N'%urgent%' starts with a wildcard, making the predicate non-sargable and forcing a full scan of Sales.Orders.
NOT IN with a subquery that can return NULL yields no rows when any NULL is present, producing incorrect results instead of the expected filter.
ISNULL(o.CustomerID, 0) = 1 wraps the column in a function call, preventing index seeks and forcing a scan.
o.OrderID + 0 = @OrderID applies arithmetic to the indexed key column, destroying sargability and blocking index matching.
o.CustomerID <> 1 uses an inequality that typically prevents efficient range seeks and can cause broad table scans.
CROSS JOIN between Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers without a filter multiplies rows exponentially and indicates a missing join condition.
Comma-separated tables in FROM with predicates in WHERE obscure join intent and make accidental cartesian products easier to introduce.
A correlated scalar subquery executes a COUNT lookup for every qualifying row in Sales.Orders instead of using a set-based join.
Correlated EXISTS runs per outer row; expressing this as an INNER JOIN or semi-join would allow the optimizer to choose a more efficient hash/merge strategy.
The identical CASE expression is duplicated in the SELECT list and ORDER BY, increasing parsing cost and maintenance risk if logic changes.
ORDER BY 2 references a column position that will break or reorder incorrectly if the SELECT list columns are rearranged.
OFFSET/FETCH with only OrderDate lacks a tie-breaker column, so rows may shift between pages when duplicate dates exist.
UNION performs an unnecessary distinct sort on two branches that are already disjoint by construction (OrderID = 1 vs 2).
COUNT(*) > 0 computes a full aggregate when only row existence is required; EXISTS would short-circuit and avoid counting.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID silently keeps the last row's value if multiple rows return, leading to unpredictable results.
@OrderIds is a table variable that will always report a cardinality of 1 to the optimizer, causing poor plan choices as data grows.
SELECT ... INTO #ImplicitOrders creates a temp table with inferred schema, preventing explicit indexing and potentially causing type mismatches.
A FAST_FORWARD cursor processes Sales.Orders row-by-row instead of using a set-based operation, degrading performance significantly.
WHILE loop iterates manually over @Counter instead of expressing the logic set-wise, adding unnecessary procedural overhead.
String concatenation builds executable SQL with @CustomerID directly embedded, enabling injection and preventing plan caching.
EXEC (@Sql) runs a dynamically built string containing unescaped user input, allowing arbitrary code execution.
String concatenation builds executable SQL with @OrderID directly embedded, defeating parameterization and plan reuse.
sp_executesql is called with a pre-built string that embeds values instead of using @parameter definitions, preventing plan caching and enabling injection.
#Scratch is created explicitly but never dropped before procedure end, leaving orphaned metadata and occupying tempdb until scope cleanup.
The CATCH block only selects the error message without rethrowing or signaling failure, silently masking runtime exceptions.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path; THROW should be preferred as it preserves the original exception context and stack.
PRINT output is buffered and not suitable for structured diagnostics or programmatic error handling in production procedures.
CustomerID = 1 is a magic business value embedded directly instead of being passed as a parameter or looked up from a configuration table.
'20160101' is an unlabelled date constant embedded in query logic, making the intent unclear and brittle to future changes.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') relies on session language settings for interpretation and can produce wrong dates or errors across locales.
COALESCE in the join predicate inhibits normal join optimization and prevents index usage on CustomerID for both tables.
Self-joining Sales.Orders to retrieve OrderID returns the same value already available from the outer alias without adding any filter.
ORDER BY o.Comments sorts by an unselective nvarchar(MAX) column, forcing a wide sort that consumes memory and disk spill.
SUM(ol.Quantity) scans the entire Sales.OrderLines fact table without a restrictive filter, causing heavy I/O and memory usage.
HAVING ol.OrderID > 0 filters individual rows after grouping; this predicate belongs in WHERE to reduce the grouped row set early.
ORDER BY OrderCount is unnecessary because COUNT(*) without GROUP BY always returns exactly one row.
MERGE has known concurrency and correctness edge cases; separate IF EXISTS/UPDATE/INSERT blocks are safer for upserts.
SET Comments = Comments writes no actual data change but still generates transaction log entries and acquires locks.
DELETE based on Quantity = -1 uses a broad business value rather than a stable key, risking accidental mass data loss.
WAITFOR DELAY blocks a worker thread intentionally instead of using asynchronous scheduling or retry logic.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is opened outside a TRY/CATCH block; if an error occurs after this point, the transaction may remain open and lock resources.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT ON changes session state and introduces fragile identity-management coupling that can cause key collisions if not carefully managed.
EXEC sp_who lacks a schema qualifier; unqualified system routines can be shadowed by user objects and cause ambiguous resolution.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 modifies session state permanently for the connection and is never restored, affecting all subsequent statements.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories VALUES (...) omits an explicit column list, making the statement fragile to schema changes and ambiguous.
String concatenation builds executable SQL with @CustomerName directly embedded, defeating parameterization and enabling injection.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated into dynamic SQL before execution, allowing arbitrary T-SQL injection.
sp_executesql executes a pre-built string that embeds values rather than using @parameter definitions, preventing plan caching.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers lacks a WHERE clause and will modify every row in the temp table, causing unintended data loss.
Predicate on o.OrderDate placed in WHERE effectively converts the LEFT JOIN to an INNER JOIN, filtering out unmatched Customers.
WHERE DeliveryMethodID = NULL uses ANSI_NULLS-compliant comparison that always evaluates to UNKNOWN; use IS NULL instead.
dbo.GetCustomerType(CustomerID) is a scalar UDF in the SELECT list that forces row-by-row evaluation and blocks parallelism.
The CATCH block inside an explicit transaction does not issue ROLLBACK before handling the error, leaving an open transaction and holding locks.
WITH (INDEX = 1) hardcodes a physical index hint, overriding the optimizer's cost-based choices and breaking if the index is renamed or dropped.
Four levels of nested subqueries (t3 -> t2 -> t1 -> base) should be refactored into CTEs or temp tables for readability and optimizer friendliness.
SELECT * on a table-valued function couples the caller to all returned columns and prevents column pruning.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti appears to be a multi-statement TVF; an inline TVF would allow the optimizer to push predicates and join efficiently.
